title: Simple Modern Outdoor Chair
summary: A dining-height slatted outdoor chair, pocket-holed throughout — the same design scales up to a taller dining-table seat with one cut-length change.
description:
This chair is built around a genuinely useful design choice: every joint is written for both a pocket-hole jig and plain self-tapping screws, so the same plan works whether or not you own the jig. It's also a chair with a dial built in — cut the legs at 20-1/2" for a standard low patio chair, or at 24" for a seat that matches a standard-height dining table, and nothing else in the cut list changes.

The seat cleat gets one detail worth respecting exactly as written: mount its pocket-hole screws on the bottom or sides of the joint, never the top. A pocket hole facing up on an outdoor chair is a small cup that collects rainwater and holds it against the screw and the surrounding wood — exactly the kind of slow rot an otherwise well-built outdoor piece dies from years later.

The chair back is built as its own flat sub-assembly (slats screwed to a base frame) and then set into the chair with a small deliberate gap above the seat's 2x2 support cleat — again for drainage, so water sheds through the seat instead of pooling where the back meets it. Seat slats get the same 1/2" spacing treatment. Cut the longest boards in the cut list first, which is what lets every other length actually come out of the shopping list's board count instead of running short partway through.

tools.note: Every joint has both a pocket-hole and a plain-screw version in this plan.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft (not in original shopping list — needed for the seat supports)
Exterior self-tapping star bit screws, 1-1/2" or longer

cutList.note: Cut at 24" instead for a dining-table-height seat.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four legs at 20-1/2", two arm rests at 27-3/4", two side supports at 24-3/4" and two front/back aprons at 20-1/2" from the 2x4. Cut three seat supports at 20-1/2" from the 2x2. Cut five back slats at 25-1/2" and five seat slats at 20" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 10 ft 2x4, one 8 ft 2x2 and three 10 ft 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.
materials: 2x2, 8 ft (not in original shopping list — needed for the seat supports)

steps.title: Build the arm-and-leg assemblies
steps.body: Build the two arm assemblies with the longer screws, or a pocket-hole jig on the 1-1/2" setting with 2-1/2" exterior pocket hole screws. Build both leg assemblies identically.
materials: Exterior self-tapping star bit screws, 1-1/2" or longer

steps.title: Join the frame
steps.body: Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes on each end of the side supports and front/back aprons, and attach them to the leg assemblies with 2-1/2" exterior pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Add the seat cleats
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 seat supports inside the chair frame with pocket hole screws, placing every screw on the bottom or the sides of the joint — never the top, which would cup and hold rainwater against the wood and screw.
materials: 2x2, 8 ft (not in original shopping list — needed for the seat supports)

steps.title: Build and set the chair back
steps.body:
Lay out the chair back slats and attach them to the chair-back base frame with the shorter screws.

Set the assembled back inside the chair, leaving a small gap above the 2x2 seat support for water drainage, and attach to the arms with the longer screws.
materials: Exterior self-tapping star bit screws, 1-1/2" or longer

steps.title: Slat the seat and finish
steps.body:
Lay the seat slats inside the chair and attach with the shorter screws, two per joint, with 1/2" spacing between slats.

Sand and finish the whole chair before it goes outside.
materials: Exterior self-tapping star bit screws, 1-1/2" or longer

images.alt: Simple modern slatted outdoor chair with wide arm rests
